source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Operating system updates are applied to your base image every 30 days.

input_row.source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
input_row.title: Ensure Operating system updates are applied to your base image every 30 days.
input_row.description: To ensure that your fleet instances have the latest Windows updates installed, we recommend that you install Windows updates on your image builder, create a new image, and then update your fleet with the new image once a month.
input_row.rationale: All fleet instances used in user streaming sessions have only the Windows and application updates that were installed on the underlying image when it was created. In addition, any updates made to Windows or to applications on the instance during the streaming session will not persist to future sessions by the same user or other users.
input_row.audit: Perform the following steps to review the Image date. From the Console 1. Log in to the AppStream 2.0 console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/appstream2 2. In the left pane click on Images 3. Select the Image Builder tab 4. Select the link for the Image builder name you wish to view. 5. In the Image builder details tab review the Created at date and the AppStream agent version. If the created at date is over 30 days old refer to the remediation below.
input_row.remediation: Perform the steps below to create an image and update it. From the Console 1. Log in to the AppStream 2.0 console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/appstream2 2. Click Images in the left pane, then Click the Image Builder tab, and Click Launch Image Builder. 3. Choose a base image. The latest base images released by AWS is recommended and selected by default. 4. Click Next. 5. Configure Image Builder, by doing the following: Internal Only - General - Name: Type a unique name identifier for the image builder. -Display name (optional): Type a name to display for the image builder (maximum of 100 characters). - Tags (optional): Choose Add Tag, and type the key and value for the tag. To add more tags, repeat this step. - Instance Type: Select the instance type for the image builder. - Network Access Points (Optional): You can create a private link, which is an interface VPC endpoint (interface endpoint), in your virtual private cloud (VPC). To start creating the interface endpoint, select Create PrivateLink. - After you create the interface endpoint, you can use it to keep streaming traffic within your VPC. - AppStream 2.0 Agent: This section displays only if you are not using the latest version of the agent. - If you are not using the latest AppStream 2.0 agent always select the option to launch your image builder with the latest agent. - IAM role (Advanced): Use existing or create a new IAM role 6. Click Next. 7. Configure Network, do the following: - Leave Default Internet Access unselected. - For VPC and Subnet 1, choose a VPC and two subnets in different Availability Zones. - For Security group(s), choose up to five security groups to associate with this image builder. - For Active Directory Domain (Optional), expand this section to choose the Active Directory configuration and organizational unit in which to place your streaming instance computer objects. Ensure that the selected network access settings enable DNS resolvability and communication with your directory. - Choose Review and confirm the details for the image builder. - Review the configuration details. Click Launch. Next Steps Install Operating system updates and install, configure and update your applications for streaming, and then create an image by creating a snapshot of the image builder instance.
input_row.justification: Verifying that operating system updates are applied to base images every 30 days requires inspecting the actual contents and patch level of the AMI or golden image. AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI) do not expose OS-level update metadata for AMIs, nor do they provide a direct method to confirm the patch status of an image. While AWS Systems Manager and Inspector can automate checks on running instances, they cannot retroactively verify the update status of the AMI itself without launching and inspecting it, which is outside the scope of a pure API-based audit.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. Identify the base AMIs used for launching EC2 instances or other compute resources. 2. For each base AMI, launch a temporary EC2 instance using the image. 3. SSH into the instance (using appropriate credentials and security group settings). 4. Run the OS-specific command to list installed updates and check the last update date (e.g., `yum history`, `apt list --upgradable`, `rpm -qa --last`, etc.). 5. Verify that all critical and security updates have been applied within the last 30 days. 6. Document the AMI ID, the date of the last applied update, and the verification result. 7. Terminate the temporary instance after verification. 8. Repeat this process for all base images in use.
input_row.default_value: By default, Windows Operating systems are set to update automatically utilizing Windows update services. Linux operating systems do not update automatically by default.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
Based on the analysis, the control requires SSH access to instances to verify the operating system update status, which is not exposed through AWS APIs. The rationale indicates that this is a check that involves OS-level inspection, specifically to verify that updates have been applied to base images every 30 days. This signifies a manual review process.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Launch the Instance:**
   - From the AWS Console, navigate to the EC2 service.
   - Identify the AMI you wish to verify (ensure it’s the base image currently in use).
   - Launch a temporary EC2 instance using that image.

2. **Connect to the Instance:**
   - Use SSH (for Linux) or Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP for Windows) to connect to the instance.
   - Ensure that security groups allow SSH/RDP access from your IP.

3. **Check for Installed Updates:**
   - For Windows:
     - Open a PowerShell or Command Prompt window.
     - Run the command: `Get-HotFix | Sort-Object -Property InstalledOn`
     - Note the date of the last applied updates and check if they are within the last 30 days.
   - For Linux:
     - Open the terminal.
     - Run the command to list installed updates:
       - Use `yum history` (for Amazon Linux/CentOS) or `apt list --upgradable` (for Ubuntu).
     - Check the last update installation dates.

4. **Document Findings:**
   - Record the AMI ID, the date of the last applied update, and whether the updates are in compliance (applied within the last 30 days).
   - Be sure to note any critical or security updates specifically.

5. **Terminate the Instance:**
   - After documenting your findings, terminate the temporary instance to avoid unnecessary costs.

6. **Repeat Process:**
   - Repeat this process for all base images in use to ensure compliance across the board. 

This process provides a thorough verification to confirm compliance with the control regarding OS updates on base images.
